## Project Analysis: Would Recorded Lecture Videos Benefit Students?

### Summary
For this project, I looked at COMP 110 survey data to see if recorded lecture videos would help students understand content better. I wanted to analyze whether the class moves too fast, and how helpful in-person lectures are. My idea was that if a lot of students think that the class is too fast or that there are hard to understand, then recorded videos could be really helpful.

### Visualizations

**Chart 1: Distribution of Student Understanding Ratings**
![Histogram of student understanding](comp110-site/static/imgs/chart1.png)

**Chart 2: Course Pace vs. Student Understanding**
![Line plot of pace vs understanding](comp110-site/static/imgs/chart2.png)

**Chart 3: Lecture Effectiveness vs. Student Understanding**
![Scatter plot of ls_effective vs understanding](comp110-site/static/imgs/chart3.png)

### Conclusions
The data suggests that recorded lecture videos would help COMP 110 students. The line graph showed that students who felt the class was moving too fast also felt like they comprehended the material less. The histogram showed that while most students rated their understanding around a 5 out of 7, many students still felt their understanding was lower (around 3 or 4). This means that there is a noticeable group of students that may be struggling in class and could benefit from recorded lecture videos. Lastly, the scatter plot showed no strong connection between how effective lectures were and how well students understood the material. Overall, recorded videos could be beneficial to students so they can go back and review the material at their own pace, especially if they feel the class is too fast paced.
